coredata.dc:title: A sensitive ratiometric biosensor for determination cardiac troponin I of myocardial infarction markers based on N, Zn-GQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Mingxing
Jiang, Rongrong
Zheng, Meie
Li, Mengjiao
Yu, Qingjie
Zhu, Hongda
Guo, Huiling
Sun, Hongmei
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive unlabeled ratiometric biosensor was developed to the detection of cardiac troponin I (cTnI). This biosensor was established by using the glassy carbon electrode coated with graphene oxide to form a platform bonded with N, Zn co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Zn-GQDs). The N, Zn-GQDs was successfully prepared as the raw materials of graphite powder and characterized. Antibodies of cTnI were bonded to the surface of N, Zn-GQDs as the nanoprobe by amide bonds. The signals of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were exposed to decrease in the presence of cTnI, which caused the signal substance to move farther away from the electrode. It was found that the immune complex layer attenuated the intensity of ECL and DPV which could be used as the good overall signal for determining concentration of cTnI. The ratiometric biosensor had a good response to cTnI with the detection limit is 4.59 pg L−1 in the concentration range of 10–106 pg L−1. The developed method was evaluated for the detection of cTnI in human serum, and the obtained results were consistent compared to the reference values obtained by hospital standard enzyme linked immunoassay (ELISA) with 9.09%–11.1% of RSD. Our findings suggested that this ratiometric biosensor could be used to the detection of cTnI in human serum with lower cost and higher sensitivity, it also might be better potential application prospect based on N, Zn-GQDs to detect other biomarkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Cardiac troponin I
Ratiometric biosensor
Biomarker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Preparation of N, Zn Co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Zn-GQDs)
Detection of cTnI in human serum
The experimental conditions optimization and stability of N, Zn-GQDs
Selectivity and stability of the immunosensor
Detection of cTnI in human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81201197 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectrum of GO and N-GQDs (A). The SEM (B) and TEM (C) image of GO. The SEM (D) and TEM (E) image of N, Zn-GQDs. The DLS of N, Zn-GQDs (F).
The fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs and N, Zn-GQDs (A). The UV–vis absorption spectra of N -GQDs and N, Zn-GQDs (B). The XPS spectra of N, Zn-GQDs (C).

PL intensity of N, Zn-GQDs in different volume ratio of N-GQDs: Zn (COO)2 (A). PL intensity of N, Zn-GQDs in different temperature (B). The size and PDI (C) and PL intensity (D) of N, Zn-GQDs in different time.
The CV (A), the EIS (B) and the ECL signals (C) curves of N-GQDs(a), N, Zn-GQDs (b), N, Zn-GQDs-anti (c), N, Zn-GQDs-anti-BSA (d), N, Zn-GQDs-anti-BSA-cTnI (e).
The ratio of ECL responses to DPV responses of immunosensor for the selectivity (A). The ratio of ECL responses to DPV responses of immunosensor in different time (B).
ECL signals (A) and DPV signal (B) of N, Zn-GQDs in the different concentrations of cTnI. The calibration curve of ECL/Current cTnI to the cTnI concentration (C).
Schematic of the prepared rout of N, Zn-GQDs (A) and the ratiometric biosensor for analysis of cardiac troponin I (B).
Comparison of the proposed ratiometric immunosensor with other biosensors for cTnI detection.
Detection of cTnI in human serum samples (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * P < 0.05 compared to this ratiometric immunosensor.

head.ce:title: A sensitive ratiometric biosensor for determination cardiac troponin I of myocardial infarction markers based on N, Zn-GQDs

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive unlabeled ratiometric biosensor was developed to the detection of cardiac troponin I (cTnI). This biosensor was established by using the glassy carbon electrode coated with graphene oxide to form a platform bonded with N, Zn co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Zn-GQDs). The N, Zn-GQDs was successfully prepared as the raw materials of graphite powder and characterized. Antibodies of cTnI were bonded to the surface of N, Zn-GQDs as the nanoprobe by amide bonds. The signals of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were exposed to decrease in the presence of cTnI, which caused the signal substance to move farther away from the electrode. It was found that the immune complex layer attenuated the intensity of ECL and DPV which could be used as the good overall signal for determining concentration of cTnI. The ratiometric biosensor had a good response to cTnI with the detection limit is 4.59 pg L−1 in the concentration range of 10–106 pg L−1. The developed method was evaluated for the detection of cTnI in human serum, and the obtained results were consistent compared to the reference values obtained by hospital standard enzyme linked immunoassay (ELISA) with 9.09%–11.1% of RSD. Our findings suggested that this ratiometric biosensor could be used to the detection of cTnI in human serum with lower cost and higher sensitivity, it also might be better potential application prospect based on N, Zn-GQDs to detect other biomarkers.

ce:section.ce:para: Heart cerebrovascular disease is the collective designation of heart vascular and cerebrovascular disease, it generally points to the ischemic or hemorrhagic disease with the heart, brain and systemic organization [1,2]. The patients with myocardial injury often have chronic or severe acute skeletal muscle injury, so early diagnosis of cardiovascular disease is particularly important [3,4]. Biochemical markers of early myocardial injury are mainly limited to creatine phosphokinase (CK), myoglobin (MB), creatine kinase SUB type MB (CK-MB) and cardiac troponin I (cTnI) [5–7]. However, CK, MB, and CK-MB exist not only in myocardial tissue, but also in non-myocardial tissue, when skeletal muscles are damaged, they also increase to varying degrees [8]. As for cTnI, it can not only show the small damage to the heart muscle, but also can remain in the heart muscle tissue for a long time [9,10]. Therefore, the detection and analysis of cTnI have important clinical research significance for the early diagnosis of heart cerebrovascular disease.

To date, various methods for detecting cTnI had been reported, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), radioimmunoassay and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [11–13]. Compared with the above methods, electrochemical immunoassay (ECIA) had many advantages of more convenient, more economical and easier miniaturized as a new analytical method [14–17]. Moreover, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) also existed the high selectivity and sensitivity. ECL involved the electron transfer reaction to form the excited state of luminescence. ECL integrated electrochemical, spectral techniques, spatio-temporal resolution and spectral resolution that make it to a powerful tool for biosensing and imaging [18–20]. However, ECL was designed on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE), so it was obvious that the process of capturing antibody leaded possibly to the inadequacies of the conjugate. Beside this, the biosensors were influenced by the operator, probe load density, non-target induced reagent dissociation and other factors, all of these might limit the frequency of their application [21,22]. Therefore, it was needful to overcome all these limitations in clinical application. The ratiometric biosensor could provide the built-in self-calibration to correct for various disturbances unrelated with the analyte, and its reproducibility could be greatly improved [23,24]. A high repeatability proportional adaptive sensor was developed for the first time based on the ratio of enhanced ECL signal to stable electrochemical signal [25]. The ratiometric biosensor had the better repeatability compared with the traditional electrochemiluminescence biosensor which had been applied to the detection of actual samples and achieved satisfactory results [26].

Graphene oxide (GO) has high specific surface area and its two-dimensional structure also allows the existence of substance on the surface. Thanks to these unique attributes, GO is a good vector in the application of biological materials [27]. GO was loaded on the surface of GCE treated with aluminum powder because of its good electrical conductivity and excellent surface ability [28,29]. Indeed, GO has been intensively used to the detection of the biomarkers with electrochemical sensor and made the good progress [30]. The production of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) based on GO was a green strategy in the development of many applications due to their outstanding properties, including superior biocompatibility, bright fluorescence and strong resistance to photobleaching [31]. But the fluorescence properties and current intensity of N-GQDs should be further improved to use for photoelectric chemistry. Zn played an important role in human growth and development, immune function and wound healing as the metal element. Beyond this, it could enhance the fluorescence and the electrical conductivity of the N-GQDs [32]. Therefore, the N-GQDs doped with Zn would have a better prospect in the application of electrochemistry and fluorescence probes.

In this paper, the simple unlabeled ratiometric biosensor was developed for the detection of cTnI. The N, Zn-GQDs was successfully prepared as the raw materials of graphite powder (Scheme 1 A). The carboxylic acid groups of N, Zn-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Zn-GQDs) were activated by EDC/NHS and spread on the surface of GO. There were the large number of carboxylic acid groups on the surface of N, Zn-GQDs, which could form the amide bond with the amino group on the surface of the antibody of cTnI. And then, cTnI could be adsorbed to the electrode through antibody-antigen specific capture [33]. The electrochemical behavior of the complex materials was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The detection of cTnI was performed by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and ECL signals. Because the immune complex on the surface affected the electron and light transfer, the ratio of the two signals could be used as the good overall signal to determine the antigen concentration (Scheme 1B). The ratiometric biosensor had the good response to cTnI in the concentration range of 10–106 pg L−1with the detection limit of 4.59 pg L−1. In addition, the developed method was evaluated for the detection of cTnI in human serum samples, and the obtained results were consistent with the reference values obtained by hospital. Our findings suggested that this ratiometric sensor could be used to the detection of cTnI in human serum with lower cost and higher sensitivity and would be the potential prospect of clinical application.

Nitric acid, potassium persulfate (K2O8S4) and concentrated hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Quinoline and graphite powder were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). L-cysteine was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Immunoglobulin G (IgG), human serum albumin (HSA) and amyloid β-protein (Aβ), bovine serum albumin (BSA), cardiac troponin antibody and recombinant cardiac troponin (cTnI) were purchased from Beijing Bioss Biological Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl was used as the electrochemical detection solution. All solutions were prepared with deionized water (DI, ≥18.2 MΩ cm) purified by Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA, USA).

UV–Vis absorption spectra was recorded on Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The particle sizes and zeta potentials were measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, England). The fourier transform infrared spectrophotometric (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Bruker IFS 55 F T-IR spectrometer (Faellanden, Switzerland). The electrochemical measurement was performed on CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China). The electrochemiluminescence (ECL) was characterized by MPI-EII instrument (Xi'an, China). The morphology of nanomaterials was characterized by Hitachi-7700 Instrument (Tokyo, Japan) and JEOL JEM-2100F instrument (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on PHI 5000 Versaprobe Ⅲ (osaka, Japan). The statistical analysis was used IBM SPSS Statistics 23.

The preparation of GO was performed as follows according to the previous report [34,35]. The mixture of 1 g graphite powder in 30 mL of sulphuric acid and 0.8 g sodium nitrate were violently agitated in the ice bath about 4 h, and then 4 g of potassium permanganate was slowly added at room temperature and stirred about 1 h. Subsequently, 100 mL of deionized water was added in the suspension at 37 °C and heated up to 95 °C for 2 h, and lastly adding 25 mL of 30% (w/v) of hydrogen peroxide to terminate further reaction. The prepared GO powder was successively washed with 5% hydrochloric acid and deionized water for several times, and dried for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-GQDs was prepared according to the literature [36,37]. 15 mg of GO powder, 45 mg of L-cysteine and 15 mL of deionized water were mixed for ultrasonic dispersion, then 100 μL of ammonia hydroxide was used to adjust the pH value to 8–9 and ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min. The dispersion mixture was transferred to the autoclave lined with muffle furnace and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. They were dispersed by ultrasonic again for 30 min after cooling to room temperature, and then filtered out the black precipitates. The supernatant was transferred to the round-bottom flask and water was removed by rotary evaporator. The yellow solid N-GQDs was obtained by freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, Zn Co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, Zn-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The 1 mL of 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs solution was accurately measured in the glass bottle and heated to 40 °C, then 2 mL of 100 μg mL−1 zinc acetate solution was slowly added drop by drop under high-speed magnetic stirring, and the mixture was reacted in avoiding light condition for 3 h [38]. After that, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. After washing with water twice, the N, Zn-GQDs were obtained and dispersed in 1.0 mL of PBS solution to acquire 1 mg mL−1 solution.

The carboxyl groups on the surface of N, Zn-GQDs was activated by agitating intensely for 2 h the mixture of 1 mL of 1 mg mL−1 N, Zn-GQDs and 1 mL PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 4 mg 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) -3-ethylcarbondiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 1 mg N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) [39].

The ratiometric biosensor was fabricated as follows: firstly, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μm aluminum slurry and cleaned in 0.05 mol L−1 H2SO4 and PBS with cyclic voltammetry for 10 cycles between + 0.6 and - 0.6 mV until a stable cyclic voltammogram was obtained. The electrode was immersed in water and ethanol in turn, ultrasonic cleaned and dried to obtain a clean bare electrode [40]. Then, 5 μL of 1 mg mL−1 GO suspension was dribbled on the electrode surface and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 5 μL of 1 mg mL−1 N, Zn-GQDs and 5 μL cardiac troponin antibody (100 μg mL−1) was modified onto the GO at 25 °C for 2 h and then washed with PBS to remove the unbound probe. Then, 5 μL of 1% bovine serum albumin (BSA) was incubated at 37 °C to seal the non-specific binding sites for 2 h. After washing with PBS incubated at 4 °C [41].

The prepared biosensor was immersed by 5 μL of cTnI at different concentrations (10, 100, 103, 104, 105, 106 pg L−1) for 12 h at 4 °C and then the detection of cTnI was performed in 0.1 mol L−1 PBS solution (pH = 7.4). The recovery rate of this biosensor was measured by adding standard recovery method. Specific operations included that the standard solution with known concentration of cTnI was added to the same serum sample and analyzed according to the same analysis steps. The ratio of the difference between the result of the spiked and the result of the unspiked was calculated as the recovery rate of the spiked sample. The Electrochemical parameter was −0.6–0.8 mV (Ag/AgC1) and the scanning rate was 100 mV/s and the condition of ECL intensity was the scanning range of −2.0- 0 V in 50 mmol L−1 K2O8S4 solution and the potential increment of 10 mV during the detection. The cyclic voltammograms (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and ECL signal were gradually displayed some differences with the change of cTnI concentration. Therefore, the change of current signal reflected directly the change of cTnI concentration [42,43]. The detection limit was detected by the different concentrations cTnI standard solution adding to the prepared sensor probe [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cTnI in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability of the proposed biosensor, the four healthy blood samples (collected from Hubei University of Technology Hospital and was approval by Scientific Research Ethics and Safety Committee of Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan, China) were tested through the prepared immunosensor and the electrochemiluminescence instrument (ELISA) in the hospital. Firstly, the blood samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min to remove cell debris. Then, the serum was collected in a new tube for later use. The content of cTnI was measured by the change of DPV and ECL signal.

GO and N-GQDs were characterized by FT-IR in Fig. 1 A. GO and N-GQDs was appeared the stretching vibration peak of carbonyl group at about 1655 cm−1 and hydroxyl group at about 3400 cm−1 which the more oxygen-containing groups were modified on the materials surface. Also, N-GQDs showed a sharp new peak of the formation of C–N bond at 1100 cm−1.

The SEM and TEM images of GO were presented in Fig. 1B and C, respectively. Clearly, GO appeared the multi-layer structure in SEM due to the stacking, but it can be seen from TEM that GO had a single-layer sheet structure. Meanwhile, it was shown that the N, Zn-GQDs was spherical morphology with uniform particle distribution and the average particle size of about 100 nm from Fig. 1D and E. However, the average particle size of N, Zn-GQDs was about 147 nm by DLS due to the hydrodynamic size from Fig. 1F.

The fluorescence emission wavelength of N-GQDs was 400 nm with green but the wavelength of N, Zn-GQDs appeared at 420 nm and the fluorescence intensity of N, Zn-GQDs was about three times that of N-GQDs in Fig. 2 A when 340 nm was used as excitation wavelength and the slit was 10 nm. The phenomenon showed that the fluorescence was attributed to the recombination of Zn and Zn improved the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence images were consistent with the fluorescence spectra obtained by the irradiation at 365 nm in UV lamp, but GO had no fluorescence at 365 nm. N-GQDs showed the ultraviolet absorption peaks at 362 nm and 976 nm, however, the N, Zn-GQDs was appeared a new ultraviolet absorption peak at 262 nm after Zn doping in N-GQDs from Fig. 2B. Also, the binding energy of Zn was emerged in N, Zn-GQDs with 1021.8 eV of the 2p3 from Fig. 2C [45]. The above results indicated that N, Zn-GQDs was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The experimental conditions optimization and stability of N, Zn-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of key experimental conditions was performed to improve further determination sensitivity by controlling variables, including the volume ratio of N-GQDs to Zn (COO)2 and the reaction temperature. Also, the stability of N, Zn-GQDs was investigated.

The content detection of the N-GQDs was calculated by using the quinoline sulfate (0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 as solvent, quantum yield = 54%) as standard [46]. The selected concentration of N-GQDs in experiment process was about 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.0, 1.4 mg mL−1. The photoluminescence (PL) was positively correlated with the concentration of N-GQDs. The fitted linear calibration equation was y = 2920.5x + 1639.1 (R 2 = 0.9992), where y represented the PL of the N, Zn-GQDs and x represented the concentration of N-GQDs.

The 1 mg mL−1 N-GQDs solution was prepared by based on the obtained standard curve of N-GQDs. By setting single factor experiment, when the recognition time of N-GQDs and Zn (COO)2 was 3 h, the PL intensity of N, Zn-GQDs reached the peak at 1:2 in Fig. 3 A. It's PL intensity reached the peak at 40 °C when the temperature rises from 25 °C to 60 °C as shown in Fig. 3B.

Within 30 days, the particle size of N, Zn-GQDs in water was changed within 1%, and the PDI remained about 0.1 (Fig. 3C). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of N, Zn-GQDs was observed to decrease about 2% (Fig. 3D), which proved that the prepared N, Zn-GQDs had excellent stability.

The fabrication process of the electrochemical sensor was further monitored by cyclic voltammograms (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M KCl. The CV was changed by analysis of the current change shown in Fig. 4 A. When N, Zn-GQDs were modified on the surface of GCE, the CV was improved than the N-GQDs, however, it was gradually reduced when antibody, BSA and cTnI were modified on the surface of GCE in turn. This phenomenon might be explained that N, Zn-GQDs was revealed the better electrochemical property than the N-GQDs and the bioactive substance on the surface of GCE reduced the electron transfer ability. The above results prompted that the biosensor of cTnI was successfully prepared.

It can be seen from the nyquist diagram (Fig. 4B) that the EIS consisted of the semicircle and the linear part, while the semicircle part showed the process of electron transport. The diameter of the semicircle was related to the electron transfer resistance (Ret). The diameter of the semicircular circle of N, Zn-GQDs was reduced compared with N-GQDs, this indicated that N, Zn-GQDs had better conductivity. As Antibody, BSA and cTnI were successively modified on the surface of GCE in turn, the diameter of the semicircular circle became larger and larger. Ret was enlarged with the increase of the semicircle diameter due to the bioactive substance blocking the conductivity. Also, the impedance of the prepared sensors increased successively. The ECL intensity was detected by cyclic voltammetry under the same optimized experimental conditions in Fig. 4C. When N, Zn-GQDs was introduced into the GCE, the ECL peak intensity was noticeably increased to 6700 a. u. which was approximately 6 times greater than the N-GQDs. The ECL intensity current gradually decreased with the decoration of antibody, BSA and cTnI in turn, because the complex conjugate of antibody-antigen blocked the propagation of light signals. It indicated furtherly that the immunosensor of cTnI was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of immunosensor, the ratio of ECL signals to DPV signals was investigated when 1 μg mL−1 of IgG, HSA and Aβ were added to the immunosensor under the same conditions for experiments (Fig. 5 A). Obviously, the value of the blank sample was largest and the cTnI exhibited significantly stronger response than three interferences, which it indicated that the biosensor had a good selectivity. Meanwhile, the prepared immunosensor must be stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C in the experimental process, and its stability was investigated by measured the ratio of ECL signals to DPV signals in this paper. The ratio of immunosensor was measured every day as shown in Fig. 5B, the obtained results indicated that it presented a good stability within 7 days.

The ECL intensities and DPV signal were measured in different concentrations of cTnI based on the prepared immunosensor in Fig. 6 A and B, which presented that the ECL intensities and DPV signal reduced gradually with the increasing concentration of cTnI. The obtained results appeared that the immunosensor exhibited a linear regression in the range of 10–106 pg L−1 from Fig. 6C with the detection limit of 4.59 pg L−1 (S/N = 3). The equation was ECLN, Zn-GQDs/Current (a.u./10−3μA) = −770.598lgcTnI+2.02699 (R 2 = 0.992), where the ECL
N, Zn-GQDs represented the ECL intensity of the N, Zn-GQDs and the cTnI represented the concentration of N, Zn-GQDs. It showed that the ratiometric biosensor had the good biological response signal. Meanwhile, the obtained results indicated that it presented lower detection limit and higher sensitivity for cTnI detection compared with other methods as shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cTnI in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from four healthy individuals. As seen from Table 2 , it was consistent of the measured values between the prepared ratiometric immunosensor and the ELISA with 9.09%–11.1% of RSD range. We compared the results of this ratiometric immunosensor with the results of the hospital used ELISA to determine statistical significance by using T test statistical method, and the result showed that there was the significant difference compared to the ratiometric immunosensor. The standard addition recovery rates were investigated to evaluate furtherly the accuracy of the ratiometric biosensor. The recovery rates were in the range of 92–97.1%. The obtained results showed that this biosensor could be regarded as the new method for quantitative detection of cTnI and might be extended to application prospect.

In summary, a highly sensitive and reliable ratiometric immunosensor was researched for the detection of cTnI based on N, Zn-GQDs as signal amplification by ECL signal and DPV signal. GO had the large surface area to load with the large amount of N, Zn-GQDs for signal amplification. The N, Zn-GQDs was successfully modified on the surface of GCE and bound to the antibody of cTnI through the amide bond. The cTnI was recognized by the specificity between antigen and antibody. At the same time, the obtained results showed that N, Zn-GQDs could improve the sensitivity and precision of the ratiometric immunosensor. Its detection limit could reach to 4.59 pg L−1 with the detection range of 10–106 pg L−1. The developed method was evaluated for the detection of cTnI in human serum, and the obtained results were consistent compared to the reference values obtained by ELISA with 9.09%–11.1% of RSD. It was shown that it could be applied to the detection of cTnI in human serum samples with the higher sensitivity and wider detection range. Also, the ratiometric immunosensor based on N, Zn-GQDs might be better potential application prospect to detect other biomarkers.

Rongrong Jiang: Methodology, Writing - original draft preparation. Mingxing Liu and Hongmei Sun: Investigation, Reviewing. Rongrong Jiang and Qingjie Yu: Experiment. Meie Zheng and Huiling Guo: Experiment, Data curation. Mengjiao Li: Experiment, Software. Mingxing Liu and Hongda Zhu: Design, Supervision.
This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81201197).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Prof. Chuangqi Feng and Wei Wen of the Analytic and Testing Center of Hubei University.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free, single protein detection on a near-infrared fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube/protein microarray fabricated by cell-free synthesis
Nano Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. H. Ahn, J. H.Kim, N. F. Reuel, P. W. Barone, A. A. Boghossian, J. Zhang, H. Yoon, A. C. Chang, A. J. Hilmer, M. S. Strano, Label-free, single protein detection on a near-infrared fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube/protein microarray fabricated by cell-free synthesis, Nano Lett. 11 (2011) 2743-2752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic performance of novel chromium oxide-silicon dioxide decorated on multi-walled carbon nanotubes and graphene oxide nanocomposites: preparation, structural, and optimization
